title: Aether — a language in your browser
description: A from-scratch ML-family programming language with Hindley–Milner inference, a kind system with higher-kinded types — genuine Functor/Applicative/Monad classes with superclasses — and THREE BACKENDS that all run the same typed program byte-for-byte: a bytecode stack VM, a JavaScript code generator, and a NATIVE WEBASSEMBLY BACKEND that hand-assembles the program into a real `.wasm` module (a from-scratch binary encoder — no wabt, no binaryen), instantiates it in the browser, and runs it — closures via `call_indirect`, constant-space TAIL CALLS (return_call), structural `match`, and ADTs/records/tuples as tagged cells, with printing/show/comparison delegated to imports that reuse the VM's own code so equivalence holds by construction. Its linear-memory heap is reclaimed by a from-scratch, precise, non-moving TRACING GARBAGE COLLECTOR (mark-sweep, hand-written in wasm) whose root finder is a SHADOW STACK codegen keeps in lock-step with the hidden wasm operand stack — a GC STRESS MODE collects before EVERY allocation and the result stays byte-for-byte identical (proving the root set is complete) while a long allocator loop holds a bounded peak heap instead of growing forever. The WebAssembly backend is also SELF-DESCRIBING: a `name` section lets it DISASSEMBLE ITS OWN BYTES back into readable WAT text via a from-scratch binary decoder (the mirror of the encoder — no wabt, no wasm2wat) that resolves every call/global/local to a `$name`, and a shared SMALL-INTEGER CACHE lets the allocator reuse cells instead of boxing fresh ones (invisible to results since every value is compared structurally), with the module counting the allocations it saved. You can download the `.wasm` and run it anywhere. All three backends compile the output of a real, multi-pass OPTIMIZING MIDDLE-END that rewrites the elaborated core to a fixpoint — constant folding + algebra, β/η-reduction, capture-avoiding inlining, dead-binding elimination, KNOWN-CONSTRUCTOR `match` reduction, record field projection and COMMON-SUBEXPRESSION ELIMINATION — so type-class abstraction MELTS AWAY (a `class` method call on a literal constructor folds all the way to a single literal) and repeated work runs ONCE. The local CSE only shares work on a single node's binder-free frontier, so a from-scratch GLOBAL VALUE NUMBERING pass (a top-down, dominator-style AVAILABLE-EXPRESSIONS analysis) shares a pure, costly expression recomputed ACROSS BINDERS — on either side of a `let`, inside a `λ` body, or across a `match` — hoisting it into ONE shared `let` at the dominating node; it fires only when the expression is guaranteed-evaluated more than once, so the VM step count can only fall, and it never moves an effect or speculates work onto a path that did not need it (a window recomputed as the value of three different lets is shared once, roughly halving a kernel's steps). Where the inliner once copied a function only when it was used exactly once, a SIZE-BOUNDED CALL-SITE INLINER now copies a small non-recursive function into EVERY saturated call site — deleting the closure-application overhead and exposing its body to const-folding (`sq 3 + sq 4 + sq 12` folds to a single `169` and `sq` vanishes) — while partial applications and higher-order ESCAPES keep one shared closure; it is monotone by construction (an inlined call runs fewer instructions and an un-taken copy costs nothing), so the harness's never-increase-steps gate proves it is never worse (a numeric kernel drops −45% of its VM steps). CSE is backed by a from-scratch INTERPROCEDURAL EFFECT-&-TOTALITY ANALYSIS that proves which functions are effect-free and total, so even a repeated *call* to a pure helper is shared — and it only ever merges effect-free expressions on a guaranteed-evaluated path, so it can never lose an effect or add a step. Its totality half is a from-scratch SIZE-CHANGE TERMINATION analysis (Lee–Jones–Ben-Amram, POPL 2001): for every call it builds a size-change graph relating the caller's parameters to the callee's arguments on the well-founded STRUCTURAL SUBTERM ORDER (a value peeled out of a constructor/cons/tuple by a `match` is strictly smaller), closes those graphs under composition per strongly-connected component, and PROVES a recursive function halts when every idempotent self-graph descends some parameter — so CSE/DCE now share or drop *recursive* pure calls (`length`, tree folds, a Peano-`Nat` factorial), while higher-order combinators and unbounded integer countdowns are honestly left unproven; a Termination panel draws each function's descending ↓ thread and the first-order call graph. The middle-end also compiles pattern matching to GOOD DECISION TREES (Maranget, 2008): instead of re-testing a shared constructor prefix once per arm, it tests each scrutinee position ONCE — a core-to-core lowering into single-column `match`es plus join-points (handling guards and non-exhaustive MATCH_FAIL) that every backend runs unchanged, with a panel that draws the tree. Every rewrite is proven semantics-preserving by the same byte-for-byte equivalence checks, surfaced in an Optimizer panel with the per-rule rewrite breakdown, a round-by-round fixpoint trace, and the VM-step reduction. Plus DERIVING `(Eq, Ord, Show, Enum, Bounded, Functor, Foldable)` synthesised from a data type's shape, property-based testing that generates inputs from inferred types and shrinks failures, monadic do-notation, list comprehensions, an interactive HM derivation tree, a time-travel debugger, and turtle graphics.
